coredata.dc:title: Mechanism of membrane formation from mixing cellulose acetate and carbon quantum dots together in different solvents
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Hong-Li
Wu, Min-Hsun
Ang, Micah Belle Marie Yap
Li, Chi-Lan
Tsai, Hui-An
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
coredata.dc:description:
Background The separation performance of a membrane mainly depends on its morphology. Casting solutions formed by different solvents have different membrane formation mechanisms, resulting in the formation of membrane with different morphology and pores.
Methods CA with/without CQDs was dissolved in various solvents, using water as non-solvent to prepare CA membrane through non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS). The dynamic behavior of membrane formation was investigated using the dope viscosity, optical microscope observation of non-solvent penetration into the casting film, and light transmittance. However, the thermodynamic behavior was demonstrated by the phase diagram and FTIR-microscopy.
Significant findings CQDs addition and suitable solvents can tailor the physicochemical properties of membrane through its formation. When CQDs were added to different solvents dissolved CA dope, the surface porosity and pore size of the membranes increased while skin layer thickness decreased. In addition to viscosity, the kinetic behavior of membrane formation was also affected by the affinity between solvent and non-solvent. FTIR-microscopy revealed that the time required for phase separation of the 2P system was longer than that of the DMSO system. This is due to the higher viscosity of the 2P system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose acetate
Membrane formation
Carbon quantum dots
Phase inversion
FTIR-microscopy

Investigation of the kinetic behavior of CA solutions
Pure water flux and MWCO of the CA membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface and cross-sectional morphology of the CA membranes. Membrane condition: (a) CA/2P; (b) CA/NMP; (c) CA/DMSO; (d) CA/DMAc; (e) CA/2P+CQDS; (f) CA/NMP+CQDs; (g) CA/DMSO+CQDs; (h) CA/DMAc+CQDs.
3D AFM images of the CA membranes. Membrane condition: (a) CA/2p; (b) CA/NMP; (c) CA/DMSO; (d) CA/DMAc; (e) CA/2P+CQDS; (f) CA/NMP+CQDs; (g) CA/DMSO+CQDs; (h) CA/DMAc+CQDs.
Water contact angle of the CA membranes.
Optical microscopy images of the coagulant penetrated the CA casting solution with different solvent system under different time.
(a) The non-solvent penetration distance in different CA casting solutions verse time1/2 and (b) Light transmittance of CA/solvents and CA/solvent+CQDs systems.
Ternary phase diagram of the (a) CA/solvent/H2O and (b) CA/solvent+CQDs/H2O systems.

Composition weight fractions of CA/2P/water system (a, b) and CA/DMSO/water system (c, d) under non-solvent (water) contact with casting solution for different times. The depth at 0 µm (a, c) and 200 µm (b, d).
Time dependence composition path of CA/2P and CA/DMSO systems at 0 µm (a, c), and 200 µm (b, d) depth beneath the surface after contacting with water. .
Composition weight fractions of CA/2P and CA/DMSO casting film after contact with water at different times. (a) 6 s, (b) 36 s, (c) 72 s, and (d) 108 s.
(a) Pure water flux, (b, c) PEO MW rejection, (d, e) Cumulative pore size distribution curves, and (f, g) Probability density function curves.
UV–VIS absorbance of the permeate of CA/NMP+CQDs membrane under long-term running.

head.ce:title: Mechanism of membrane formation from mixing cellulose acetate and carbon quantum dots together in different solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: R&D Center for Membrane Technology and Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: R&D Center for Membrane Technology and Department of Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: R&D Center for Membrane Technology and Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The separation performance of a membrane mainly depends on its morphology. Casting solutions formed by different solvents have different membrane formation mechanisms, resulting in the formation of membrane with different morphology and pores.

CA with/without CQDs was dissolved in various solvents, using water as non-solvent to prepare CA membrane through non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS). The dynamic behavior of membrane formation was investigated using the dope viscosity, optical microscope observation of non-solvent penetration into the casting film, and light transmittance. However, the thermodynamic behavior was demonstrated by the phase diagram and FTIR-microscopy.

CQDs addition and suitable solvents can tailor the physicochemical properties of membrane through its formation. When CQDs were added to different solvents dissolved CA dope, the surface porosity and pore size of the membranes increased while skin layer thickness decreased. In addition to viscosity, the kinetic behavior of membrane formation was also affected by the affinity between solvent and non-solvent. FTIR-microscopy revealed that the time required for phase separation of the 2P system was longer than that of the DMSO system. This is due to the higher viscosity of the 2P system.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane separation technology, a green technology, has become an in-demand separation process in these years. This is because of the increasing global awareness of the society in protecting the environment. Compared with the conventional separation techniques, membrane separation has the advantages of low energy consumption, excellent separation performance, low cost, and few or no secondary pollution [1,2]. As a result, intensive research was done on the field of membrane technology for industrial applications [3]. Membranes separation processes, such as microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF), and reverse osmosis (RO) process, are mainly categorized by their pore size of the membrane [4].

The current UF membranes have lower separation efficiency [5,6]. Controlling its membrane structure can tailor its separation performance [7]. This can be done through changing either its porosity, pore size, thickness of the skin layer, and surface roughness [8]. Thus, identifying the formation mechanism of the membrane can help to tune the membrane morphology.

UF membranes are commonly fabricated through non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) technique [9], where a porous membrane can be easily obtained. During NIPS, kinetic and thermodynamic behavior of the polymer solution affects the morphology of the membrane. The observing of solvent and non-solvent exchange rate in the polymer solution by rheometer, optical microscope, and light transmission experiment, etc. can be used to illustrate the dynamic behavior of the membrane formation process [10]. On the other hand, the thermodynamic behavior can be investigated by the position of the binodal curve in the ternary phase diagram of polymer/solvent/non-solvent [10,11].

The composition or temperature of polymer solution and coagulation bath are important parameters for membrane formation [9,12,13]. The most convenient method is to adjust the composition of polymer solution, since changing the composition of the coagulation bath is not practical. The composition of polymer solutions can be adjusted by varying polymer concentration [14,15], solvent [15,16], and additive [17,18].

Typically, the ultrafiltration membranes have porous macrovoids and a dense skin layer. These porous macrovoids provide low mass transfer resistance, whereas the dense skin layer has high mass transfer resistance [19]. Membranes with high mass transfer resistance reduce the membrane permeability. Therefore, it is important to investigate the formation mechanism of the membrane during membrane formation.

The top layer morphology of membrane consists of two elements, the surface pore size [20] and skin layer thickness [21,22]. Adding additive is the efficient and simple way to tune the membrane morphology through NIPS [23,24]. By adding hydrophilic additive to prepare membranes, porous top surface and thin skin layer can be easily obtained [24,25], while the membrane have high flux and separation efficiency.

The composition of the polymer solution is a critical parameter for membrane formation and determining the membrane structure. Specifically, changing the solvent in the polymer solution is one of the effective ways to tune the membrane morphology and thus affect the separation performance. Kahrs et al. [26] studied the effect of different solvents on the viscosity, membrane morphology, permeability, and mechanical stability of the polyethersulfone membrane. A suitable solvent was found to produce membranes with high permeability and appropriate mechanical properties. Dong et al. [27] investigated the effect of N-methly-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), dimethyl acetamide (DMAc) and PolarClean on the property of polysulfone membrane. It was observed that using different solvents had an effect on morphology, membrane performance, molecular weight cutoff (MWCO), and hydrophilicity. Ma et al. [28] used different solvent (dimethyl formamide, DMAc, NMP) to dissolve poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-chlorotrifluoroethylene) and obtained different membrane porosity and performance. Thankamony et al. [29] utilized the mixed solvent phase separation (MSPS process) to study the effect of solvent and upper critical solution temperature on fabricating polyvinylidene fluoride/cellulose acetate (CA) membrane. Kim et al. [30] explored the acetone as a co-solvent with the ionic liquid to prepare cellulose membrane. They showed that the use of co-solvent had a large impact on membrane morphology, MWCO, membrane performance, and mechanical stability.

Different organic and inorganic additives, with hydrophilic property, has been added to polymer solution for investigating their effect in membrane formation. These organic additives were polyvinylpyrrolidone [28,31] and polyethylene glycol [31,32]. However, in recent years have been embedded of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), ZnO [33,34], silicon dioxide [33,35], titanium dioxide [36,37], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [38,39], graphene oxide (GO) [35,40] and other inorganic additives in the membrane. CQDs, CNT, and GO have zero-, one-, and two-dimensional carbon-based structures, respectively [41,42]. Researchers have already employed CQDs in nanofiltration [43,44], forward osmosis [45,46], reverse osmosis [47,48], and pervaporation membranes [49]. Nevertheless, the membrane formation of adding CQDs in different composition of polymer solution for NIPS process has been not widely investigated in literatures [50,51]. In our previous work [51], the effect of CQDs incorporation mechanism on membrane formation was investigated. In short, according to the observation of the phase diagram, the thermal stability decreased after the addition of CQDs, indicating that the CQDs system is more prone to phase separation. Thus, the membrane becomes more porous. Furthermore, incorporation of CQDs into the CA polymer solution resulted in the formation of a hydration layer due to the high hydrophilicity of CQDs during membrane formation. As a result, the hydration layer creates a higher driving force for water to enter the polymer solution. Porous membranes are easily obtained when higher non-solvent (water) flows into the polymer solution. Meanwhile, in this previous study, we also used FTIR analysis to show the presence of CQDs in the CA membranes.

The top surface morphology of CA/NMP membranes was also systematically tuned by different content of CQDs in our previous work [51]. It was found that the morphology of the top layer was dominated by thermodynamic behavior. Subsequently, a porous and thin top layer was fabricated. This would be ideal for the UF membrane to improve the membrane flux. Since different solvent systems would affect the formation mechanism of the membrane, resulting in the formation of different morphology. Therefore, it is necessary to further explore the role of CQDs in polymer solutions in different solvents. Therefore, in this work, CQDs were added to different CA solvents such as 2-pyrrolidone (2P), NMP, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and DMAc. Among these solvents, although 2P is a relatively rare solvent, because its properties are very similar to NMP, it is an organic compound derived from γ-butyrolactone. Its chemical formula is C₄H₉NO, and its melting point and density are 245–250 °C and 1.128 g/cm³ (at 20 °C), respectively. Some scholars in the literature have also used it for membrane formation [52,53,54], so 2P was selected as one of the solvent options in this study. The use of different solvents to prepare CA membranes with different morphology was studied, and the membrane formation mechanism of different solvent systems was also discussed.

CA powder (CA-394–60S) was procured from Eastman Chemical Company (CA, USA). NMP, DMAc, DMSO, 2P, all reagent grade, were the solvents used for dissolving CA powder. NMP and DMAc were purchased from Tedia Company Inc. (Fairfield, OH, USA). DMSO was purchased from Echo Chemical Co. Ltd. (Taipei, Taiwan). 2P was purchased from Acros Organics (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA). Citric acid (anhydrous) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Showa Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Polyester nonwoven was bought from Ahlstrom (Helsinki, Finland). Poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) 35k, PEO 100k and PEO 300k were provided by Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, MO, USA). PEO 600k was supplied by Acros Organics (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA).

The synthesis of CQDs is similar to our previous work [51]. Briefly, citric acid was pyrolyzed at 210 °C for 3 hr. Afterwards, the solution was neutralized, filtered through a 0.22 µm filter, and dialyzed for 6 hr with 1000 Da dialysis tubing. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried overnight to obtain the CQDs.

The CA membrane was fabricated by non-solvent induced phase separation process. Table 1 lists the composition of the polymer solutions used in this work. 1000 ppm of CQDs was dispersed in different solvent using a homogenizer at a speed of 12,000 rpm for 30 min, and then the CA powder was dissolved into the solution at 90 °C, where the concentration of CA was fixed at 13 wt%. Afterwards, the CA solution was cast on top of glass covered with polyester non-woven, and immediately immersed in water coagulation bath (25 °C) for phase separation. Finally, the membranes were washed several times to remove the residual solvent and stored in water until use.

Surface and cross-sectional morphology of the membranes were captured using field-emission scanning electron microscope (Model S-4800, Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan). The dried membranes were placed on the SEM sample holder using carbon tape. For cross-sectional morphology, samples were freeze-fractured under liquid nitrogen. The pore size and surface porosity of the membrane were determined using Image J software. Four samples were measured for each membrane condition. The surface roughness of the membrane was measured using atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA), in terms of root-mean-square (Rq).

The C50–1 spindle of rheometer (Brookfield R/S-cps, MA, USA) was used to measure the viscosity (η) of the polymer solution at 25 °C. The titration method was used to examine the cloud-point measurement of the polymer solution at 25 °C. Light transmission experiments (Model: XFDC-100, Hangzhou Yaobo Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Zhejiang, China) were used to study the demixing rate of polymer solutions. The polymer solution was cast on a glass plate, and then immediately immersed in the water coagulation bath at 25 °C to observe the light transmittance changes of the cast film at different times.

An optical microscope was used to record the penetration of the non-solvent (water) into the polymer solution. The polymer solution was placed between two glass slides. A drop of distilled water containing rhodamine B was introduced using a syringe to the side of the glass slide. Afterwards, it diffused into the polymer solution. The diffusion and travelled distance of the water were recorded. Diffusion coefficient (D) of the water was calculated using Fick's law as shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
d =
4 D t
where d was the penetration distance (cm) of water into the polymer solution at time t (sec).
The composition of the CA/solvent/H2O solution at different depth beneath the surface of casting film during phase inversion process was analyzed by FTIR-microscope (Perkin Elmer, Model LE186–0078).

Membranes were placed inside a membrane module and then assembled them in the cross-flow filtration set-up. Afterwards, the set-up was set to 1.5 bars for 1 h, for pre-compaction of the membranes. The pure water flux (PWF, J) was determined at 1 bar using the Eq. (2):
(2)
J =
V
A t
where V (L) was the volume of the permeate at time t, and A was the effective area (12.57 cm2) of the membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane porosity and mean pore radius
ce:section.ce:para:
The wet weight (mw ) and dry weight (md ) of free-standing membranes were measured using analytical balance. The membrane thickness (l) was recorded using the thickness gage (Teclock, YSC, Japan). The excess water was removed from the membranes while weighting the mw . The membranes were freeze dried for 4 h to make sure the membranes were completely dried, then the md was obtained. The porosity (ε) was calculated using the Eq. (3):
(3)
ε
=
m w
−
m d
l × ρ × A
where ρ and A were density of water and membrane surface area, respectively.

The Guerout–Elford–Ferry equation (Eq. (4)) was utilized to calculate the membrane mean pore radius (rm):
(4)
r m
=
(
2.9 − 1.75
ε
)
8 η l Q
ε
× A ×
Δ
P
where ε was membrane bulk porosity, η was the water viscosity (8.9 × 10−9 Pa·s), l was the wet membrane thickness, Q was the volumetric flow rate, A was the membrane surface area and ΔP was the transmembrane pressure (0.1 MPa).

A feed containing 50 ppm of various molecular weights (35k,100k, 300k, 600k) of PEO aqueous solution was prepared separately. Feed and permeate concentration were measured using the total organic carbon (Vario TOC select: TOC/TNb Analyzer, Elementar, Langenselbold, Germany). The molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) was defined by the 90% rejection of the PEO. The PEO rejection (R) was calculated by Eq. (5):
(5)
R
( % )
=
C f
−
C p
C f
×
100
where Cf was the feed concentration of the PEO solution and Cp was the permeate concentration of the PEO solution. The Stokes radii of solute (r) were calculated by applying the Eq. (6) [55]:
(6)
For
PEO
(
MW > 100 , 000
)
, r = 10.44

10
− 12
× M
W
0.587
where r was the solute Stokes radii (m) and MW was the molecular weight (kg/mol).

The solute rejection (R) was expressed as the solute size by log-normal probability function (Eq. (7)):
(7)

where R was the solute rejection (%), ds was the solute diameter, μs was the geometric mean pore diameter of solute at R = 50%, σg was the geometric standard deviation about μs .

The geometric standard deviation (σg ) was defined by the Eq. (8):
(8)

R = 84.13 %

R = 50 %

The solute rejection (R) versus solute diameter (ds ) was plotted by a log-normal probability coordinate, and the linear line was plotted by using Eq. (9):
(9)

( R )

)

The probability density function (Eq. (10)) was differentiated from Eq. (7).
(10)
d R

[
−

)

σ p
)
2
]
where dp was pore diameter, σp was geometric pore standard deviation, μp was geometric mean pore diameter (σp and μp can be considered as same as σg and μs , respectively).

The addition of hydrophilic CQDs increased the surface porosity and pore size of the membranes (Fig. 1e–h, Table 2). The kinetic and thermodynamic behavior of the polymer solution was affected by the hydrophilic CQDs [51]. Furthermore, the thickness of the skin layer became thinner for membranes with CQDs, this is because of the faster phase separation of the polymer solution top surface with CQDs than the polymer solution without CQDs [21,22]. Therefore, in the following section, the kinetic and thermodynamic behavior of different solvent system of CA solutions with and without CQDs addition are further investigated.

The bulk porosity and mean pore radius of the CA membranes are listed in Table 2. Membranes containing CQDs had relatively smaller bulk porosity than membranes without CQDs. Compared with the CA polymer solution without CQDs, CA solution containing CQDs had higher viscosity, so the solvent and non-solvent exchange rate would be reduced, resulting in low bulk porosity [51]. However, membranes with CQDs had a larger mean pore radius, which was also larger than the surface pore size. This is because the mean pore radius was measured in wet state (obtained from filtration velocity method), whereas the surface pore size was measured in dry state (obtained from SEM images). During drying of the membrane, the pores of the CA membrane were prone to shrink, thus smaller pore size was measured in SEM images.

The 3D AFM images of CA membranes with/without CQDs in different solvent systems are shown in Fig. 2 and its corresponding surface roughness are tabulated in Table 3 . The CQDs added CA membranes have relatively rougher surface than the membrane without CQDs addition. This is because the addition of CQDs makes the membrane surface more porous [56]. The CA/2P membrane have higher surface roughness due to the poor interaction between CA and 2P, which leads to easy entanglement of polymer chains [57]. Therefore, as shown in Table 3, the CA/2P and CA/2P+CQDs exhibited higher roughness than other polymer solution.

Fig. 3 presents the water contact angle of the CA membranes. The water contact angle of the membrane is governed by both the chemical and physical properties of the top surface. Membrane surface containing hydrophilic functional groups such as oxygen and nitrogen would have lower water contact angle. In terms of physical properties, hydrophilic membrane with rougher surface would also have lower water contact angle [58]. In all solvent systems, there was a slight decrease in the water contact angle when CQDs was embedded in CA membrane (Fig. 3). These results might be due to that only a slight increase in surface roughness after adding CQDs into the membrane (Table 3). However, membrane prepared using DMSO as the solvent had relatively higher water contact angle compared with other membranes, because it also had the relatively smoother surface (Fig. 2 and Table 3). Thus, there was a smaller area for water to contact in the membrane, giving a higher water contact angle. Therefore, using different solvents affects membrane surface morphology and water contact angle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the kinetic behavior of CA solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The viscosity of a casting solution can affect the phase separation of the casting film during membrane formation through its kinetic behavior. It can be seen from Table 1 that the viscosity of 2P system was higher than that of other solvent systems regardless of whether CQDs was added or not. In general, the viscosity of the polymer solution increased with friction between polymer segments. There was a weak interaction between 2P and CA polymer, therefore, there is higher friction between its segments [57]. Furthermore, all CA solutions with CQDs had higher viscosity than those without CQDs due to the formation of hydrogen bonding between CA and CQDs [51].

The penetration behavior of non-solvent (water) into CA solution was observed using optical microscopy. Fig. 4 shows the images of water penetration in different CA casting solutions at different times observed by optical microscope. The penetration distance (D) was plotted with a square root of time (t1/2), as shown in Fig. 5 a. The penetration front is governed by two factors, the affinity between solvent and non-solvent and the viscosity of the polymer solution [21]. When the affinity between solvent and non-solvent is high, the penetration front will increase. And when the viscosity of the polymer increases, the penetration front decreases.

Table 4 summarizes the diffusion coefficient of the non-solvent into the CA solution. Regardless of whether CQD was added or not, in different solvent systems, the diffusion coefficient of the non-solvent into the CA solution followed the following order: DMSO > DMAc > NMP > 2P It can be found that in the 2P, NMP and DMAc solvent systems, their diffusion coefficient increases with decreasing viscosity. This is because the mass transfer resistance of non-solvent diffusion into the casting solution increases with the increase of viscosity. Although the viscosity of the DMSO system is higher than that of the DMAc system, it has the fastest diffusion coefficient compared to the other three solvent systems. Therefore, in addition to the viscosity, the affinity also affects the diffusion coefficient of the non-solvent in the casting solution.

Solubility parameters can be used to describe the interaction between the two components. A low value of solubility parameter difference (|Δδ1–2|) indicated a strong interaction between the two components. Herein, the interaction between solvent and non-solvent shows the mixability of solvent and non-solvent [59]. The solubility parameter difference between solvent and non-solvent (|ΔδNS-S|) can indicate the exchange rate of solvent and non-solvent during membrane formation. A lower |ΔδNS-S| means faster exchange rate of non-solvent and solvent. Moreover, the interaction between polymer and solvent shows how soluble is the polymer in solvent [11]. Thus, the solubility parameter difference between solvent and polymer (|ΔδP-S|) implies the thermostability of polymer solution, where low |ΔδP-S| shows high thermostability. The CA/DMSO system has the highest diffusion coefficient. Table 5 shows that the DMSO has the lowest |ΔδNS-S|, indicating that DMSO has good miscibility with water.

Except for the CA/2P+CQDs system, the diffusion coefficient of all solvent systems declined after adding CQDs in the polymer solution. It is because the viscosity increased after the CQDs addition, which affects the diffusion coefficient of non-solvent to the polymer solution. The CA/2P system and CA/2P+CQDs system display similar diffusion coefficient. Because both systems have high polymer viscosity.

The demixing rate of CA solution can be obtained by light transmission experiment. The demixing rate increases with the decrease of light transmittance of casting film. The light transmittance of CA casting films with/without CQDs are shown in Fig. 5b. Obviously, the demixing rate follows the following sequence: CA/DMAc ≥ CA/DMSO > CA/NMP > CA/2P The reason for this phenomenon should be related to the viscosity of CA solution. Higher viscosity makes the exchange rate of solvent and non-solvent slower, resulting in lower demixing rate. Furthermore, the CA/DMAc, CA/DMSO, and CA/NMP systems show the typical instantons demixing, while the CA/2P system exhibits delay demixing. Therefore, as shown in Fig. 1, the macrovoids structure and the dense structure were found respectively. The porous surface and thinner skin layer membrane morphology was mostly caused by the rate of non-solvent inflow, which was faster than rate of solvent outflow (kinetic behavior) [21,22] and the larger demixing gap (thermodynamic behavior) [15,61]. Consequently, the phase separation process would be further investigated by the thermodynamic behavior of the polymer solution through the cloud point experiment.

The thermal stability of the CA solution is depicted in the thermodynamic phase diagram of Fig. 6 . It can be found that, regardless of whether CQDs is added or not, the amount of non-solvent required for the CA solution to reach the cloud point curve is in the following order: 2P ≈ DMSO < DMAc < NMP. These trends are consistent with the solubility parameter difference between polymer and solvent (|ΔδP-S|) shown in Table 5. From Table 5, the |Δδt,P-S| value follows the sequence: CA/2P > CA/DMSO > CA/DMAc ≈ CA/NMP, which supported the data in the cloud point curve, where the thermodynamic stability decreased with increasing |Δδt,P-S| value [11]. Therefore, the CA/2P and CA/DMSO system had lower requirement for the amount of non-solvent than the other systems because of higher |Δδt,P-S| value.

From the data in Fig. 6, it can be seen that the thermodynamic behavior of the membrane formation cannot fully explain the cause of the morphology formation of the CA membrane. Herein, the membrane formation mechanism of the CA membrane is further described by using FTIR-microscopic observation. Herein, the two solvent systems of 2P and DMSO, which are close to the cloud-point curve in the thermodynamic phase diagram, are selected to further describe the membrane formation mechanism of the CA membrane through the observation of the FTIR microscope. Fig. 7 shows the ternary component weight fractions of CA, solvent (2P or DMSO) and water during membrane formation at 0 and 200 µm depth beneath the surface, as analyzed from FTIR microscopy observations.

For the 2P system, as shown in Fig. 7a, when water was in contact with the cast film for 36 s, the 2P weight fraction on the surface of cast film decreased from 0.87 to 0.55; while water increased from 0 to 0.28. When the contact time continued to increase, the weight fraction of 2P and water remained almost unchanged. Similarly, in the DMSO system (as shown in Fig. 7c), when water was in contact with the cast film for 24 s, the weight fraction of DMSO on the cast film surface decreased from 0.87 to 0.47; while water increased from 0 to 0.47. When the contact time continued to increase, the weight fractions of DMSO and water remained almost unchanged. As for the weight fraction of solvent and water at the position of 200 µm below the casting film surface, it can be observed in Fig. 7b and d. Due to the high viscosity of the 2P system, when water was in contact with the casting film for 96 s, the 2P weight fraction at 200 µm depth beneath the surface slightly decreased from 0.87 to 0.81, while the water slightly increased from 0 to 0.04. In the lower viscosity DMSO system, when water was in contact with the casting film for 72 s, the weight fraction of DMSO at 200 µm depth beneath the surface decreased from 0.87 to 0.70, while that of water increased from 0 to 0.18. Since the viscosity of the 2P system is much higher than that of the DMSO system, it takes a long time for the 2P system to keep the weight fraction of the solvent and water on the surface of the casting film constant after the water is in contact with the casting film. And inside the film (at 200 µm depth beneath the surface), due to the viscosity, the exchange rate between the solvent and the non-solvent is different. Therefore, after the 2P system contacts the water, the slower exchange rate makes the change of the weight fraction of the 2P and water slower than that of the DMSO system. This can also be confirmed from the diffusion coefficient of water in the CA casting solution in Table 4.

Fig. 8 displays the time dependence composition paths of CA/2P and CA/DMSO systems at 0 and 200 µm depth beneath the surface after contacting with water. The critical composition (✶) is defined as the composition close to the cloud point curve. Due to the detector interval limit is 1.1 s, the CA/2P system at 0 µm and CA/DMSO system at 200 µm show two critical composition point, respectively. Indicates the time between two critical compositional points of a composition approaching the cloud point curve. The time required for the composition path through the cloud point curve after contacting the water with cast film are listed in Table 6 . On the cast film surface, the time for the compositions of CA/DMSO system and CA/2P system to pass through the cloud point curve was 3.2 s and between 22.9 s and 23.9 s, respectively. At the 200 µm depth beneath the surface, the time for the composition of the CA/DMSO system to pass through the cloud point curve was between 41.3 s and 42.3 s, while that of the CA/2P system was 112.1 s. These phenomena mean that when the casting film comes into contact with water, it takes longer time for the CA/2P system to phase separate than the CA/DMSO system. This might be because the water in the CA/2P system is inhibited by the viscosity, resulting in a slower penetration rate into the casting film, so it takes a longer time for phase separation to occur.

Further observe the composition weight fraction changes of the two casting films after contacting water at different times (as shown in Fig. 9 ). It is obvious that after the two systems were in contact with water, the water weight fraction at 200 µm depth beneath the surface was less than that on the cast film surface (0 µm) at any time. However, the solvent weight fraction at 200 µm depth beneath the surface was greater than that on the cast film surface. After 6 s of contact with water (Fig. 9a), the water weight fraction of CA/DMSO system on the cast film surface was 0.26, significantly higher than that of the CA/2P system which was only 0.026. After 36 s (Fig. 9b), the water weight fractions of the CA/DMSO system and the CA/2P system on the cast film surface increased to 0.47 and 0.276, respectively, and after 108 s (Fig. 9d), the water weight fractions were 0.488 and 0.277, respectively. It is evident that there is little change in the water weight fractions of the two systems between 36 s and 108 s.

For the composition fraction at a depth of 200 µm beneath the surface, when the CA/DMSO cast film was in contact with water between 6 s and 36 s, the water fraction increased while DMSO decreased, but there was little change between 72 s and 108 s. In the CA/2P system it took more than 72 s for the water fraction to increase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pure water flux and MWCO of the CA membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 a shows the pure water flux of the CA/solvent+CQDs membranes. It can be found that the pure water flux of CA membranes with different solvents follow the following order: DMAc > DMSO > NMP > 2P Moreover, all membranes containing CQDs had a higher pure water flux than membranes without CQDs. These results were aligned with the membrane bulk porosity and mean pore radius, where the pure water flux increased with porosity and mean pore radius (Table 2).

Fig. 10b and c are the rejection of the PEO for the CA membranes without CQDs and with CQDs, respectively. The membrane MWCOs obtained from these two figures are listed in Table 7 . The MWCO of the membrane with CQDs is higher than that without CQDs.

Fig. 10d and e present the pore with size less than stated (%) vs pore size of the CA membranes. The geometric mean pore diameter and geometric pore standard deviation (Table 7) were obtained from R = 50% and Eq. (8) (R = 50% and R = 84.13%). CQDs contained membranes had higher geometric mean pore diameter than CQDs-free membranes. Regardless of whether CQDs is added or not, the geometric mean pore diameter of the membrane in different solvent systems is as follows: DMAC > DMSO > NMP > 2P This trend can correspond to the viscosity of the casting solution in order: DMAC < DMSO < NMP < 2P (as shown in Table 1), illustrating that kinetics behavior might dominate the membrane formation. Therefore, after membrane formation, DMAC system has the largest mean pore diameter, while 2P system has the smallest mean pore diameter.

Fig. 10f and g show the pore size distribution of CA membranes. The mode pore diameter listed in Table 7 were obtained from the highest peak of probability function. The mode pore diameter of all membranes is smaller than the geometric mean pore diameter, indicating that the membranes have many extremely large pores. In particular, the membrane of the 2P system has a smaller mode pore diameter. Therefore, the lower PEO rejection was caused by the large pores of the CA/2P membrane. Subsequently, the membranes of the 2P system exhibited higher MWCO.

In order to test the stability of CQDs after long-term running, the CA/NMP+CQDs membrane was operated for 24 h under the pressure of 1 bar with deionized water as the feed. The obtained permeate was analyzed by UV–VIS spectroscopy to observe whether the CQDs were leached from the membrane. Fig. 11 shows the results of UV–VIS spectroscopy analysis of DI water, 1000 ppm CQDs solution, and permeate obtained at different operating times. From Fig. 11, it can be observed that the absorbance of permeate and DI water was very similar, indicating that CQDs should not be leached from the CA membrane during long-term running.

The stability and long-term of the UF membrane might be determined by its antifouling property, chemical resistance, and mechanical property. For UF membranes prepared from the same polymer in different solvents, basically, the antifouling properties and chemical resistance are not much different. However, the membranes prepared by different solvents have different morphology types, so the mechanical properties of the membranes are quite different.

UF membranes with bicontinuous morphology or sponge-like morphology would have higher mechanical stability. However, UF membranes with marcovoids morphology might collapse due to the compaction of the feed during operation. Therefore, in this study, we though that the CA membrane prepared with 2P as solvent would have better mechanical stability against impact.

Asymmetric CA membranes with/without CQDs incorporated were prepared through non-solvent induced phase separation by using various solvents dissolved CA solution and water as the casting solution and coagulant, respectively. The membrane formation mechanism was investigated systematically. Compared with other solvents, when CA was dissolved in 2P, the CA casting solution has a very high viscosity due to the weak interaction between 2P and CA polymer, resulting in a low solvent/non-solvent exchange rate during membrane formation, thus forming a denser and lower surface porosity CA membrane. The positions of the cloud-point curves of the two systems in the phase diagram are almost the same for 2P and DMSO. However, due to viscosity effects, FTIR-microscopy observations show that the compositional path of the DMSO system takes less time to cross the cloud-point curve than the 2P system, both at the surface and at a depth of 200 µm below the surface. Furthermore, the time difference of the composition path of the DMSO system from the surface of the cast film pass through the cloud-point curve to the depth of 200 μm beneath the surface pass through the cloud-point curve is also shorter than that of the 2P system. All the membranes prepared by adding CQDs had higher pure water flux and MWCO than those without CQDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan (MOST 110-2221-E-033-012) for supporting this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2023.105082.
